noteP1: We picked fifteen companies from our portfolio that map to the priorities of The White House's technology organization: frontier AI and the infrastructure to run it economically, the data foundations underneath, and the security and developer tooling that let both move to production.
noteP2: Each card opens to a brief and the angle we think fits The White House best, and you can filter by category. Our full portfolio of 150+ active companies is linked in the top right. Whenever it's convenient, send back the ones worth a closer look.
portfolioIntro: Every company here works at the infrastructure layer, across AI, data, security, and developer tooling, with an angle on where it fits The White House's agenda. Filter by category, then expand any card for the brief.

anthropic.pull: The AI program at The White House sits inside the center of a large federal technology ecosystem that must coordinate agencies, critical infrastructure and national-security missions under strict records and security rules. In that setting, a frontier model has to be useful across knowledge work and software delivery without creating an unmanageable decision surface. Anthropic gives the technology organization one platform for reasoning over policy memoranda, agency reports, intelligence assessments, legislative text, public comments and cyber findings, orchestrating bounded agents and assisting development across policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications. Claude's focus on steerability and predictable behavior is especially relevant where national security, civil liberties, records retention, model evaluation and supply-chain trust cannot be bolted on later. The result would be a safer path from isolated copilots to faster government decision support with evidence, control and public accountability intact.
cases: Reason across policy memoranda, agency reports, intelligence assessments, legislative text, public comments and cyber findings without forcing teams into a separate knowledge system.
Use Claude Code across policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications and the integration services behind them.
Keep human review, model policy and audit evidence explicit in high-consequence workflows.

wispr.pull: The information gap at The White House is not always a missing database; often it is what policy staff, technologists, cyber teams, legal counsel, agency leaders and national-security professionals know but do not record during briefings, stakeholder meetings, field reports, hotline interactions and interagency coordination notes. Wispr Flow lets users speak naturally into existing desktop and mobile tools, producing structured, edited text with commands and filler-word cleanup. That makes it well suited to distributed or frontline work around policy development, agency coordination and cyber-response workflows. The technology organization gains more timely operational context without asking teams to adopt a separate note-taking system or spend the end of a shift reconstructing what happened.
cases: Turn briefings, stakeholder meetings, field reports, hotline interactions and interagency coordination notes into structured entries while the context is still fresh.
Work inside policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications rather than creating another application to manage.
Create a richer decision trail for handoffs, analytics and supervised agents.

browserbase.pull: A meaningful share of The White House's work still crosses agency portals, regulatory sites, public data sources, interagency tools and legacy federal web systems. Those browser-bound steps are hard to automate reliably because they carry session state, changing interfaces and sensitive credentials. Browserbase matters as secure, programmable browser infrastructure for agents: it can let the technology organization automate web workflows and test policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications at scale without building a brittle fleet of headless browsers. The fit is strongest for repeatable, supervised tasks inside policy development, agency coordination and cyber-response workflows, where centralized sessions, logs and controls are as important as the automation itself.
cases: Automate bounded tasks across agency portals, regulatory sites, public data sources, interagency tools and legacy federal web systems without waiting for every system to expose an API.
Run repeatable browser tests across policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications before customer- or employee-facing releases.
Centralize sessions, credentials and execution logs for governed agent activity.

coder.pull: As The White House modernizes policy development, agency coordination and cyber-response workflows, the development environment itself becomes part of the platform strategy. Coder moves workspaces off individual laptops and into cloud infrastructure where images, access, compute and network paths can be standardized. That would help engineers working across policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications reproduce one another's setup, adopt AI coding tools within guardrails and scale specialized compute without permanent endpoint upgrades. For the technology organization, the benefit is higher engineering throughput with tighter source-code control and clearer unit economics.
cases: Standardize cloud workspaces for teams building policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications.
Keep source code, credentials and approved AI tooling inside controlled infrastructure.
Reduce onboarding and environment drift across distributed, acquired or specialized teams.

token.pull: For the technology organization, AI-agent governance becomes an identity problem before it becomes a model problem. The White House is the center of a large federal technology ecosystem that must coordinate agencies, critical infrastructure and national-security missions under strict records and security rules; adding service accounts, agency integrations, automation bots and policy or cyber agents increases the number of principals acting at machine speed. Token Security provides the discovery, ownership, least-privilege and audit layer required to keep that population controlled. Applied across multi-agency cloud, secure-enclave and high-assurance computing environments, it can show where credentials are over-permissioned or orphaned and make access changes traceable. That gives The White House a controlled path toward faster government decision support with evidence, control and public accountability intact and avoids an invisible tier of privileged automation.
cases: Inventory service accounts, agency integrations, automation bots and policy or cyber agents and tie every credential to an accountable owner.
Constrain agent access to the specific policy, agency, cyber, program, public and national-security data required for each workflow.
Preserve lifecycle and entitlement evidence for audits, regulators and internal risk review.

fal.pull: The next digital experiences at The White House may need to generate or transform public information, training simulations, multilingual media and national-security visualization assets in real time. fal gives engineers an inference platform built for those media-heavy workloads, abstracting accelerator scheduling and model serving behind straightforward APIs. It would complement policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications by making multimodal capabilities easier to embed and scale. The fit is especially useful for narrow, reviewable workflows that advance faster government decision support with evidence, control and public accountability intact—while brand, privacy, safety or regulatory controls remain in the surrounding application rather than being assumed from the model alone.
cases: Embed image, video, audio or voice generation into new AI, cyber, citizen-service and interagency coordination capabilities.
Prototype against multiple media models through one developer-facing inference layer.
Keep review, consent and brand or safety controls in the surrounding application workflow.

montecarlo.pull: The White House is the center of a large federal technology ecosystem that must coordinate agencies, critical infrastructure and national-security missions under strict records and security rules, so a small defect in policy, agency, cyber, program, public and national-security data can propagate across many users and workflows. Monte Carlo matters because it extends data observability into the model and agent layer rather than treating each as a separate monitoring stack. The technology team could trace an unreliable output back through pipelines, transformations and code, then prioritize remediation by downstream consequence. This is particularly relevant to policy development, agency coordination and cyber-response workflows, where the cost of a silent issue is measured in service, safety, revenue or trust—not only in a failed job.
cases: Monitor policy, agency, cyber, program, public and national-security data from source through dashboards, models and agent actions.
Trace incidents to the affected policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications and accountable engineering teams.
Prevent silent data failures from undermining faster government decision support with evidence, control and public accountability intact.

tonic.pull: For The White House, better test data can improve both delivery speed and production safety. Tonic can create high-fidelity synthetic versions of citizen, agency, policy, program and security data, allowing developers to exercise policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications against realistic volumes and relationships without relying on raw production data. The same approach can support model training, fine-tuning and evaluation for policy analysis, cyber coordination, secure knowledge work and high-assurance agents. The strategic fit is strongest where data provisioning is slowed by national security, civil liberties, records retention, model evaluation and supply-chain trust, yet inadequate test coverage would expose the organization to even greater operational or customer risk.
cases: Replace production copies of citizen, agency, policy, program and security data in development and test environments.
Generate rare and edge-case scenarios for policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications and AI evaluation.
Reduce privacy exposure while giving teams faster access to useful data.

netbox.pull: The White House is the center of a large federal technology ecosystem that must coordinate agencies, critical infrastructure and national-security missions under strict records and security rules. That scale creates a persistent infrastructure-data problem: asset records, topology and configuration intent are often split across tools and local teams. NetBox Labs can centralize that context for federal networks, secure enclaves, cloud services, agency connections and critical-infrastructure interfaces and expose it programmatically to operations and automation. That is useful for everyday network changes, but also for the agentic future—the safest infrastructure agent is one grounded in an authoritative model of devices, sites, addresses and dependencies. For The White House, that supports faster change with less operational ambiguity.
cases: Create a maintained source of truth across federal networks, secure enclaves, cloud services, agency connections and critical-infrastructure interfaces.
Drive provisioning and change validation for multi-agency cloud, secure-enclave and high-assurance computing environments from intended state.
Ground infrastructure automation and agents in accurate topology and ownership data.

valar.pull: As The White House develops new AI, cyber, citizen-service and interagency coordination capabilities, model serving can become a hidden bottleneck: teams compete for accelerators, batch jobs wait, and each application invents its own routing logic. Valar centralizes those concerns inside the customer's VPC. By optimizing asynchronous, batch and agentic inference, it can support policy analysis, cyber coordination, secure knowledge work and high-assurance agents with better utilization and policy consistency. The strategic value for the technology organization is control—capacity, prompts, outputs, logs and models stay inside the same boundary used for multi-agency cloud, secure-enclave and high-assurance computing environments.
cases: Run policy analysis, cyber coordination, secure knowledge work and high-assurance agents inside The White House's own VPC and security boundary.
Increase usable accelerator capacity through scheduling, batching and routing.
Keep prompts, outputs, logs, models and policies under enterprise control.

echo.pull: The applications supporting policy development, agency coordination and cyber-response workflows depend on a deep open-source and container supply chain. Echo matters because it replaces vulnerable building blocks with maintained, hardened dependencies that can be consumed as standard inputs. That gives developers working on policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications a safer starting point and reduces the patch churn that pulls security and platform teams away from higher-order risks. It also strengthens auditability: the organization can show which approved components were used and how they are maintained across the lifecycle.
cases: Standardize hardened dependencies across secure collaboration, policy, cyber, public-service and AI applications.
Remove known vulnerabilities before they enter application and agent build pipelines.
Use FIPS-validated options where stronger assurance is required.

act.pull: The White House's current agenda—accelerating federal adoption of frontier AI, high-security computing and post-quantum cyber capabilities while emphasizing evaluation, accountability and civil liberties—will increase the number of connections among applications, data services and AI agents faster than traditional firewall review can keep pace. Act Security maps those relationships and highlights communication that is unnecessary or inconsistent with business purpose. Applied to multi-agency cloud, secure-enclave and high-assurance computing environments, it could guide safe, incremental perimeter changes around the systems handling policy, agency, cyber, program, public and national-security data. The result is enforceable least privilege that reflects the real communication paths behind policy development, agency coordination and cyber-response workflows, rather than a static policy that becomes obsolete after the next release.
cases: Map intended and actual communication across multi-agency cloud, secure-enclave and high-assurance computing environments.
Create least-privilege perimeters around policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications and their data services.
Reduce lateral-movement risk without deploying another host agent.

clover.pull: The White House's technology agenda spans new AI, cyber, citizen-service and interagency coordination capabilities, each with different data, identity and operational assumptions. Clover Security gives product-security teams a way to review that intent directly from planning and design materials instead of waiting for a scan at the end. It can flag missing requirements, route decisions to accountable owners and keep the rationale attached to the work item. That supports faster government decision support with evidence, control and public accountability intact by making secure design part of the normal delivery motion across policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications, not a separate process teams learn to avoid.
cases: Review new AI, cyber, citizen-service and interagency coordination capabilities while requirements and architecture are still being shaped.
Map security decisions back to tickets, design documents and accountable owners.
Reduce late-stage rework across policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications without adding another approval queue.

drata.pull: The White House has to prove trust across federal records, privacy, accessibility, procurement, zero trust and national-security controls while its technology estate and third-party relationships continue to change. Drata matters because it turns controls, evidence, risks and assurance signals into a continuously monitored trust graph rather than a periodic collection exercise. The technology organization could use it to connect policies and owners to the systems behind policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications, surface control drift and reuse evidence across overlapping reviews. That makes compliance more operational and gives leadership a current view of the risks that could slow progress toward faster government decision support with evidence, control and public accountability intact.
cases: Continuously map controls and evidence across policy, records, secure collaboration, cyber, public-service and interagency applications and multi-agency cloud, secure-enclave and high-assurance computing environments.
Reuse assurance work across federal records, privacy, accessibility, procurement, zero trust and national-security controls instead of rebuilding it for every review.
Give leaders a current view of control drift, ownership and third-party risk.

orca.pull: Cloud and AI assets at The White House will change faster as the organization pursues its current agenda: accelerating federal adoption of frontier AI, high-security computing and post-quantum cyber capabilities while emphasizing evaluation, accountability and civil liberties. Orca Security discovers that estate agentlessly, maps the relationships among workloads, identities, data and vulnerabilities, and ranks risk by likely impact. Applied to multi-agency cloud, secure-enclave and high-assurance computing environments, it can give security and platform teams a shared picture of the exposures affecting policy, agency, cyber, program, public and national-security data. The result is fewer blind spots, less alert fatigue and a remediation plan grounded in actual attack paths—all in service of faster government decision support with evidence, control and public accountability intact.
cases: Gain agentless visibility across The White House's cloud estate and emerging AI workloads.
Prioritize attack paths into sensitive The White House data and critical applications.
Unify posture, workload, data, vulnerability and compliance remediation in one view.
